summary: Old completeLogin's catch handler unconditionally nulls this.pendingLogin even after a second login() has already replaced it, making every subsequent ensureAuthenticated() throw 'Login failed: AbortError'.
failure_scenario: User calls login() twice. Session 2 sets pendingLogin = () => completionPromise2. Session 1's fetch is aborted, its catch fires and sets pendingLogin = null and pendingLoginError = AbortError. Any list_chats call now sees pendingLogin===null + pendingLoginError set and throws 'Login failed: AbortError' — session 2 is abandoned with no way to recover short of restarting the server.

summary: loginAbortController.signal is wired into every request() call, so calling login() aborts all in-flight listChats/getMessages fetches via the old controller.
failure_scenario: listChats is awaiting a response. A second login() fires, calls loginAbortController.abort() on the old controller. The in-flight listChats fetch receives AbortError and throws; the tool call surfaces 'Failed to list chats: AbortError' instead of results.

summary: refreshIfExpired() has no mutex; concurrent MCP tool calls both see the token as expiring and both POST to tokenRefresh, racing to save the result.
failure_scenario: Two tool calls arrive back-to-back (MCP stdio transport fires handlers concurrently without awaiting each). Both read the same refreshToken and POST to tokenRefresh. The server rotates the refresh token on the first response; the second POST sent the now-invalidated token. Whichever saves second writes a stale/invalid refreshToken to disk, breaking all future cold-start sessions.

summary: completeLogin() reads this.pendingCertificate via live this-access rather than a local capture, so a concurrent login() call can overwrite it before verifyCertificate runs.
failure_scenario: Session 1 is long-polling checkQrCodeVerified. A second login() fires, overwrites this.pendingCertificate (e.g. with null if auth was cleared by a prior error), and returns. Session 1 then calls verifyCertificate with the wrong certificate value, forcing a PIN flow when the stored certificate should have been accepted.

summary: parseInt(m.createdTime) produces NaN if createdTime is absent or non-numeric, and new Date(NaN).toISOString() throws RangeError, failing the entire get_messages batch.
failure_scenario: LINE API returns a system message or a message with a missing/empty createdTime field. parseInt('') === NaN; new Date(NaN).toISOString() throws RangeError. The outer catch at line 101 returns isError:true with 'RangeError: Invalid time value', losing all messages in the batch rather than just the malformed one.

summary: Bare catch {} on verifyCertificate treats transient network errors and 5xx responses as 'certificate rejected', silently forcing a PIN flow when the stored certificate should have been accepted.
failure_scenario: Server returns HTTP 500 during verifyCertificate due to a transient outage. pinRequired stays true. The code proceeds to createPinCode, surprising the user with an unexpected PIN prompt. No log is emitted distinguishing this from the intended 'no certificate' case, making it undiagnosable.

summary: fs.appendFileSync('/tmp/line-debug.log') on every request writes access tokens and full message content to a world-readable path with no size cap.
failure_scenario: In a long-running deployment the log grows unboundedly. Access tokens, refresh tokens, and private message content are written in plaintext to /tmp; in a container with a read-only filesystem or full disk, the server crashes on the first HTTP call.

summary: fetchContactsV2 uses sequential await inside a for loop, serialising batch requests that are fully independent.
failure_scenario: A user with 200 contacts incurs 4 sequential HTTP round-trips (~300 ms each = ~1.2 s) where all four requests are independent and could complete in ~300 ms with Promise.all.

summary: mimeType is hardcoded as 'image/jpeg' for all images regardless of what the LINE CDN actually serves.
failure_scenario: LINE CDN serves a PNG sticker or WEBP profile picture. The MCP client receives it labelled 'image/jpeg', causing decoding failures or silent misrenders in any client that respects the declared MIME type.

summary: get_image happy-path test silently returns (passes with zero assertions) when no image messages are found, making it a permanent false-green.
failure_scenario: Test account contains only text messages; imagePreviewUrl stays null. The test exits at line 82 with no assertions. A regression in getImageBuffer (wrong URL, auth failure, corrupt base64) is never caught by CI.
